title: What We Talk About When We Talk About Dissipative Quantum Chaos
authors: Lucas Sá, Pedro Ribeiro, Sergey Denisov
abstract: Dissipative quantum chaos is an emerging theory that is expected to extend the ideas, concepts, and methodology of conventional Hamiltonian quantum chaos from coherent evolution to open quantum dynamics. The new theory should provide a set of tools to distinguish chaotic open quantum systems from integrable ones, as well as quantitative measures of their chaoticity (or, conversely, integrability). The foundations of this theory were laid in the late 1980s, and from the very start it was clear that, like its Hamiltonian predecessor, it had to be based on the spectral properties of the operators governing open quantum evolution. After these first steps, the field remained relatively quiet for many years and it is only over the last decade that the development of dissipative quantum chaos has received a strong boost, as confirmed by a large number of publications on this topic and, very recently, the first experiments performed to test its theoretical predictions. In this chapter, we review these recent developments and outline the basic foundations of dissipative quantum chaos.
summary: This paper reviews the emerging field of dissipative quantum chaos, focusing on how the spectral properties of open quantum systems can identify chaotic versus integrable dynamics. It connects non-Hermitian random matrix theory with physical models like the Lindblad equation to provide tools for characterizing non-unitary evolution. The work is significant for its ability to bridge the gap between quantum-to-classical transitions and the study of many-body open systems.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish a theoretical framework for dissipative quantum chaos (DQC) by extending the concepts of Hamiltonian quantum chaos to open quantum systems, specifically focusing on distinguishing between integrable and chaotic regimes in non-unitary dynamics.
main_result: The authors review the recent revival of DQC, demonstrating that the spectral properties of Lindbladians and CPTP maps (such as level spacing statistics and complex spacing ratios) serve as robust signatures of chaos, while also addressing the nuances of the quantum-to-classical correspondence.
method: The study utilizes Random Matrix Theory (non-Hermitian ensembles like Ginibre), spectral analysis of Lindblad operators, semiclassical approximations (such as the Truncated Wigner Approximation), and free probability theory.
model_or_system: The review covers various models including the Lindblad master equation (GKLS form), CPTP maps, the dissipative quantum kicked rotor/top, and many-body systems like the Dicke model and Bose-Hubbard dimers.
key_observables: Spectral statistics (level spacing distributions, complex spacing ratios, cubic-level repulsion), steady-state density operators, Wigner/Husimi functions, and photon-emission statistics.
important_parameters: Dissipation strength (Gamma), Hamiltonian vs. dissipative weight (alpha), number of decay channels (rank R), and the semiclassical limit (large spin S or particle number N).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes Markovian dynamics (Lindblad form) and relies on the validity of non-Hermitian Random Matrix Theory as a proxy for universal chaotic behavior.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the relationship between Lindblad operators, CPTP maps, and quantum states, highlighting how spectral properties are used to construct measures of chaos. Figure 4 shows the spectral density of random Lindbladians under varying Hamiltonian weights.
paper_structure: The paper follows a logical progression from the fundamental definitions of DQC and its historical development to the mathematical frameworks of Lindbladians and CPTP maps, followed by symmetry classifications, spectral signatures of chaos, semiclassical limits, and finally addressing challenges and discrepancies in the theory.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive synthesis of how dissipation modifies the signatures of chaos, which is crucial for understanding decoherence and many-body dynamics in NISQ devices, ultracold atoms, and superconducting qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Objects of dissipative quantum chaos. Spectral properties of Lindblad operators L, CPTP maps Φ, and quantum states ρ(t) (especially asymptotic steady states ρss) are used to construct measures of integrability/chaoticity. Recently, ensembles of quantum trajectories generated by different unravelings [25, 37] of the same time-continuous open evolution have also started to be used for the same purpose. Note that all these objects are interrelated. For example, a density operator can be obtained by averaging over the ensemble of unravelled trajectories, and an important subclass of CPTP maps [38] is formed by maps ΦM that can be represented as ΦM = eL·1.

Fig. 2 Reproduction of the results reported by Grobe, Haake, and Sommers in 1988 [39]. (a) Motion of the complex eigenvalues of the stroboscopic map (7) as the damping strength Γ is varied from 0 (green circles) to 0.4 (blue circles) in steps of 10−4. The eigenvalues are obtained for the even-parity sector in the chaotic regime. The parameters are S = 6, p = 2, k0 = 10, k1 = 8. (b) Integrated nearest- neighbour spacing distribution I(s). The spectra of the map are computed for S = 10, Γ = 0.1, p = 2, and 100 equidistant values of k0 ∈[10, 12]. The regular case corresponds to k1 = 0, while the chaotic one corresponds to k1 = 8. The distribution for the regular regime is compared with the...

Figure 2 presents our reproduction of the key results reported in Ref. [39]. The left panel shows the dynamics of the eigenvalues of the stroboscopic map under a gradual increase of the dissipation strength Γ, in the chaotic regime. The eigenvalue trajectories reveal nontrivial dynamics, including collisions and the consequent formation of a cluster around zero (see Section 4.2 for a related discussion). These results served as inspiration for a recent work [42]. The right panel presents the main finding (as discussed above). These results are of foundational importance for DQC and therefore deserve an even more detailed discussion; see Section 9. Next year, in 1989, Grobe and Haake coined...

Fig. 3 Spectral density of random Lindblad operators in the purely dissipative limit. (a) Rescaled eigenvalues ℓ′ = N(ℓ+ 1) of a single random Lindblad operator for N = 50 (the stationary eigenvalue ℓ1 = 0 is omitted). The solid contour shows the asymptotic boundary of the universal lemon-shaped support, Eqs. (13) and (14). (b,c) Probability density P(Re ℓ′, Im ℓ′) of the rescaled eigenvalues. Panel (b) presents the density obtained by sampling 104 random Lindblad operators for N = 100, while panel (c) shows the results obtained with the random-matrix model, Eq. (12). The dark blue contours indicate the lemon-shaped support. Note that the real eigenvalues were discarded when sampling random...

Fig. 4 Spectral density of random Lindblad operators, Eq. (8), for different values of the weight α of the Hamiltonian contribution [69]. The panels show the density of the rescaled eigenvalues ℓ′ = N(ℓ+ 1). Panel (a) corresponds to the case α = 1/2, where the spectral support is expected to become a disk. Panel (b) shows the result for α = 1, which should be compared with the result for the RMT model shown in panel (c).
